title: Signals

jokes.text: Selina corrects herself mid-sentence on the phone with Andrew: 'You were the best thing to ever happen to him and he threw it away. Yeah, you were the best thing ... I was the best thing that ever happened to you and you just threw it away.'
jokes.setup: Selina is in a heated phone argument with her ex-husband, trying to deliver a cutting line about her own worth.
jokes.punchline: She accidentally says 'him' instead of 'you,' momentarily losing track of who she's insulting, then self-corrects — revealing how flustered and self-absorbed she is simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The self-correction is a quick, economical character beat — Selina is so furious she loses her own grammar, which is both humanizing and revealing of her ego. The joke is in the stumble and the immediate recovery, very true to the character.

jokes.text: Gary asks mid-argument whether Selina wants a clutch; she says yes, then no, then yes — all while continuing to fight with Andrew on the phone.
jokes.setup: Selina is furiously dressing down her ex-husband on the phone.
jokes.punchline: Gary's mundane accessory question interrupts the tirade and Selina's distracted, contradictory answers reveal her multi-tasking chaos.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep layering — the trivial runs parallel to the emotional, deflating both. Gary's oblivious service role is perfectly captured. Not a standout gag but efficiently executed.

jokes.text: Selina says Andrew has 'successfully learned how to manipulate Catherine' — delivered as a congratulatory insult to the person she's been arguing with.
jokes.setup: Selina is furious that Andrew is undermining her relationship with their daughter.
jokes.punchline: She delivers a mock-congratulations that is more revealing of her own jealousy and insecurity than a genuine critique of Andrew.
jokes.explanation: A decent character beat that shows Selina's pettiness, but it's fairly standard sarcasm for her. Competent rather than inspired.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I hope we have a number for a contract killer, too.' Gary: 'I'll do it.'
jokes.setup: Selina vents that she needs a contract killer to deal with Andrew; it's a throwaway dark joke.
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately volunteers himself with complete earnestness, revealing his devotion has no moral limit.
jokes.explanation: Gary's instant, sincere offer is a perfect encapsulation of the Gary-Selina dynamic. The economy is exceptional — two words ('I'll do it.') does enormous character work. The joke earns its 8 because it's character-specific (only Gary could say this), structurally tight, and the dark absurdity is maximally compressed. Genuinely impressive micro-gag.

jokes.text: Mike's car joke: 'Not quite. Like your mother, it's been previously loved and paid for by a couple of guys.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks if Mike's car is new, complimenting it.
jokes.punchline: Mike delivers a surprisingly sharp 'your mom' joke with an unexpectedly sophisticated framing — 'previously loved and paid for by a couple of guys.'
jokes.explanation: A dressed-up 'your mom' joke that works because of its elevated phrasing. 'Previously loved and paid for by a couple of guys' is a memorably polished delivery of a lowbrow concept. Gets extra credit for Mike, whose jokes often feel slightly off — this one actually lands cleanly, which is a minor subversion of his character.

jokes.text: Mike's debt rationalization: 'Oh, the old drink your way out of alcoholism approach. I like it.'
jokes.setup: Mike has bought a boat to cheer himself up despite being 100 grand in debt, comparing it to not fixing a problem by making it worse.
jokes.punchline: Dan names the logical fallacy with the perfectly apt 'drink your way out of alcoholism' analogy.
jokes.explanation: Sharp analogy with clean structure. Dan's amused cruelty is perfectly calibrated. The joke has genuine intellectual wit — the analogy is apt and punchy. Good economy, character-appropriate for Dan's sardonic efficiency.

jokes.text: Mike's escalating downgrade of his drinking problem: 'Not a problem. Uh, an issue. Not even an issue. A hitch. A drinking hitch.'
jokes.setup: Dan asks if Mike had a drinking problem; Mike defensively reframes it downward.
jokes.punchline: The escalating minimization arrives at the absurd non-word construction 'a drinking hitch' — a phrase that means nothing and everything.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured escalation in reverse — the more Mike insists it's minor, the more absurd it sounds. 'Drinking hitch' is an inventive non-phrase that reveals someone in denial. The structure earns points because each step is slightly funnier than the last. Quotable, character-true, and economically devastating.

jokes.text: Dan: 'I get that all the time.' Someone: 'No, you don't.'
jokes.setup: The aide dismisses Dan instantly after he admits he didn't read the paper.
jokes.punchline: Dan tries to save face by claiming this happens to him regularly; an immediate flat denial deflates him.
jokes.explanation: A tiny but effective deflation beat. Maximum economy — five words, total denial. Works as a quick punctuation mark on the previous absurdist gag.

jokes.text: Selina describes Kent as 'that autistic lumberjack son of a bitch.'
jokes.setup: Selina needs to find Kent for the hostages meeting and is impatient.
jokes.punchline: The insult combines a clinical descriptor, a rugged occupation, and profanity in a way that is oddly precise and revealing of how Selina actually sees this data-focused robotically calm man.
jokes.explanation: The compound insult 'autistic lumberjack son of a bitch' is a genuinely original construction. Each component adds: 'autistic' speaks to Kent's robotic affect, 'lumberjack' his physical bluntness, 'son of a bitch' pure Selina fury. The combination is specific to this character relationship, quotable, and reveals Selina's frustrated admiration of Kent's impassivity.

jokes.text: Selina to Kent: 'Hey, I heard you were doing Pilates. I didn't know you had a core.' Kent: 'I do and it's like steel.'
jokes.setup: Selina makes a joke implying Kent has no emotional or physical core — a two-level insult (emotional/physical).
jokes.punchline: Kent takes it completely literally and confirms his physical core is steel, missing the jab entirely.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep double-meaning where Kent's literal reading defeats Selina's irony. The joke works because Kent's character is so consistently robotic that his straight-faced response is funnier than any comeback. The setup is elegantly double-layered (core = emotional + physical), and his 'It's like steel' is perfectly, absurdly on-brand.

jokes.text: Selina: 'You know, it's just so great talking to you, Kent. I love being close to the inaction.'
jokes.setup: Kent has explained that 'doing nothing' is the most positive thing they can do on the hostages, delivered with bureaucratic certainty.
jokes.punchline: 'Close to the inaction' is a wordplay on 'close to the action' — Selina's frustration rendered as a pithy verbal jab.
jokes.explanation: A clean, efficient piece of wordplay. Not revolutionary but sharp and well-placed. 'Inaction' lands because it crystallizes the entire bureaucratic frustration of the scene.

jokes.text: Kent corrects 'SM-17' to 'SM-17d. There's a lowercase D.' Then: 'A misplaced decimal point can kill a diabetic.'
jokes.setup: An aide says 'SM-17' when the correct designation is 'SM-17d.'
jokes.punchline: Kent's correction is delivered with terrifying gravity, then he pivots to a chillingly unrelated medical fact about decimal points and diabetics — his mind connects details to lethal consequences with the same flat affect.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The diabetic line is a perfect Kent Davison moment: the non-sequitur is deeply logical to a man who processes everything as data with lethal implications, but the jump from missile designations to diabetics is absurdly jarring. The joke earns 8+ because it is character-specific in the deepest sense — only Kent would make this connection, and only his flat delivery makes it land. It's both funny and slightly chilling. Quotable, original, and maximally compressed.

jokes.text: Kent's passive-aggressive 'That's if you can possibly be back in time' — implying Selina won't make it to the 4:30 meeting without confirming why.
jokes.setup: Kent has just confirmed the 4:30 national security meeting.
jokes.punchline: He drops a subtle undermining comment without explanation, forcing Selina to ask what she's missing — a power move disguised as politeness.
jokes.explanation: A well-executed cringe beat — Kent's subtle condescension is perfectly in character and the audience enjoys Selina's immediate confusion. Good character comedy, less quotable but effective in the scene.

jokes.text: Jonah describes the North Carolina Pork Board pig picking; Selina asks 'Sounds classy. Will Kate Middleton be there?'
jokes.setup: Jonah describes a rural pig picking as if it's a prestigious event.
jokes.punchline: Selina's fake-sincere 'Will Kate Middleton be there?' skewers the absurdity of her being asked to attend.
jokes.explanation: Functional Selina snark. The Kate Middleton reference is a bit of an easy class-signifier joke, but it's delivered well and sets up the pig-fucking line.

jokes.text: Jonah: POTUS wants to 'utilize your people skills to kick off U.S., Hey.' Selina: 'That's what they're gonna call it?' Jonah: 'Yeah.' Selina: 'Who came up with that incredibly shitty name?' Jonah: 'That would be me, ma'am.'
jokes.setup: Jonah pitches the program name 'U.S., Hey' with apparent pride.
jokes.punchline: When Selina asks who came up with the name, Jonah admits it was him — capping her contempt with his cheerful self-incrimination.
jokes.explanation: The name 'U.S., Hey' is genuinely funny as an object in itself — it captures the emptiness of political branding perfectly. Jonah cheerfully admitting he created it is the perfect cap. His obliviousness to how bad it is is perfectly in character. Well-constructed beat.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Okay, so they want me to go to a pig roast to meet a bunch of men who probably took turns to fuck the pig before they roasted it.' Jonah: 'I wouldn't presume they took turns.'
jokes.setup: Selina rants about the indignity of attending a pig roast for rural voters.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's correction implies not that pig sex didn't happen, but merely that 'taking turns' is an unverified assumption — simultaneously prudish and more disturbing than silence.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. Selina's original line is already transgressive and funny, but Jonah's response is the joke. Rather than denying the premise (pig sex), he corrects only the logistics ('I wouldn't presume they took turns') — which both confirms his obliviousness and, more devastatingly, implies the pig sex happened but was not sequential. The construction is near-perfect: Jonah's earnest procedural mind is applied to the most absurd possible subject. It's character-specific, maximally compressed, and subverts the setup in the most unexpected direction. Absolutely earns 8.5+ on craft.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I will not have you undermine me with Catherine... I want Catherine to spend Thanksgiving with me, not some random boyfriend. And what about poor Memaw, who's guaranteed to be dead by March?' Gary: '(quietly) With any luck.'
jokes.setup: Selina is fighting for Catherine's Thanksgiving attendance, invoking Memaw's imminent death.
jokes.punchline: Gary quietly mutters 'With any luck' — implying he also wishes Memaw dead, delivered so softly it's almost under his breath.
jokes.explanation: A devastating dark aside. Gary's whispered agreement is unexpected and darkly funny — it reveals both his loyalty to Selina (he hates whoever she hates) and perhaps his own complicated feelings about family. The quietness of the delivery is what makes it: it's not a showboating line, it's a barely-heard confession. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Selina says she wants Superman-like time reversal: 'I'm going to that 4:30 meeting even if I have to get Sue to fly around the world the wrong way like Superman and reverse time.'
jokes.setup: Selina is furious she might miss the national security meeting.
jokes.punchline: Her threat escalates to the physically impossible — referencing the Superman movie plot as if it's a viable logistics option.
jokes.explanation: A solid absurdist escalation. The Superman reference is unexpected and reveals Selina's pop-culture-soaked determination. It's funny because she's dead serious. Competent-to-good.

jokes.text: Gary offers to help with the air base logistics; Selina shuts him down: 'Gary. This is not your area of expertise. It's just typing into a calendar.'
jokes.setup: Gary tries to contribute to a scheduling conversation.
jokes.punchline: Selina's dismissal is brutally specific — she doesn't just say he's wrong, she reduces Sue's entire job to 'typing into a calendar,' which is also a dig at Sue.
jokes.explanation: The accidental double dismissal (of Gary AND Sue simultaneously) is good comedy economy. The specificity of 'typing into a calendar' is the right kind of belittling precision.

jokes.text: Gary's accidental sarcasm followed by instant panic: 'Oh ... I'm sorry, Sue. Your job is really difficult.' Sue: 'Excuse me?' Gary: 'You do it very efficiently.'
jokes.setup: Gary accidentally voices Selina's snark toward Sue, then immediately realizes his mistake.
jokes.punchline: His panicked correction — 'You do it very efficiently' — is so hollow it makes everything worse.
jokes.explanation: A well-executed cringe beat. Gary accidentally channels Selina's contempt, then his terrified backpedaling into the stiff compliment is perfectly characterized. The joke is in his inability to recover gracefully — 'efficiently' is the most damning possible compliment.

jokes.text: Selina: 'We got to go. We're gonna go mix with the hicks.' Then Selina on Furlong: 'Okay, well, let's see, I'd rather set fire to my vulva.'
jokes.setup: Amy says Furlong wants face time with Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's refusal uses a vivid, extreme self-harm metaphor — setting fire to her own vulva — as the preferred alternative to dealing with Furlong.
jokes.explanation: A standout Selina line. 'I'd rather set fire to my vulva' is body-specific, extreme, and entirely in her register — no other character on any show says this. The anatomical specificity is what elevates it above generic 'I'd rather die' phrasing. Highly quotable, character-defining, and delivered with perfectly calibrated contempt. Earns its 8.

jokes.text: Amy describes Furlong: 'Roger Furlong trying to play nice is like Brando trying to play Annie.'
jokes.setup: Amy explains that Furlong is trying to be pleasant for political reasons.
jokes.punchline: The simile of Brando playing Annie is vivid and specific — the most method of actors attempting the most saccharine of musicals.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, well-constructed simile. The cultural specificity — Brando's menacing intensity versus Annie's sunniness — makes the image very funny. Mike's 'That's a good one' and Selina's 'Thanks, Mike' inadvertent credit-stealing is a nice bonus beat.

jokes.text: Selina responds to Amy's Brando/Annie line: 'That's a good one, Mike.' Mike: 'Thanks, ma'am.' — Selina accidentally credits Mike for Amy's joke.
jokes.setup: Amy delivers the Brando/Annie line.
jokes.punchline: Selina misattributes it to Mike, who accepts the compliment, and Amy has no recourse.
jokes.explanation: A quick, perfect comedy beat about workplace invisibility. Amy is consistently brilliant and unrecognized — Selina's casual misattribution and Mike's shameless acceptance is very true to both characters. Fast and efficient.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Did you know that I'm paying 65 grand a year to that school? They had better be putting gold on that popcorn instead of butter, right?'
jokes.setup: Catherine's essay on a Palestinian film has gone viral; Selina is outraged.
jokes.punchline: Selina's reaction pivots from politics to economics — her metric for educational value is whether they put gold on the popcorn, a brilliantly absurd consumerist frame for intellectual output.
jokes.explanation: The gold-popcorn metaphor is a nice bit of Selina's unique mindset — reducing a political crisis to a transaction. Good character specificity and a funny image.

jokes.text: Selina: 'See, this is why China regulates their internet.'
jokes.setup: Selina learns Catherine's essay has ended up on someone's blog
jokes.punchline: A Democratic Vice President instinctively endorsing Chinese internet censorship as a parenting tool
jokes.explanation: A precision satire joke. Selina's political cognitive dissonance — liberal politician approving authoritarian censorship because it's personally convenient — is Veep's central satirical engine in one line. The non-sequitur connection (blog → Chinese internet policy) is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Dan's Pilates studio infiltration confessional: 'Well, I think I might have found a way to get to Kent Davison.' Mike: 'If you kill his mother and then dress in her skin, that's technically illegal.' Dan: 'Technically.'
jokes.setup: Dan claims he has a strategy to get to Kent.
jokes.punchline: Mike's suggestion — killing and wearing Kent's mother's skin — is insanely dark, but Dan's one-word response 'Technically' implies he considered it before dismissing it on legal grounds only.
jokes.explanation: This is a top-tier joke in the episode. The Hannibal Lecter-level suggestion from Mike is dark enough to be shocking, but it's Dan's 'Technically' that makes it exceptional. That single word confirms Dan would consider murder as a strategy if it weren't technically illegal — it's a perfect encapsulation of his amoral opportunism in one syllable. Maximum economy. Character-defining. The word 'technically' doing the heavy lifting of an entire character portrait is great craft.

jokes.text: Dan's talking-head about the regionality paper — cut to him at Kent's Pilates studio: 'Did you just join this Pilates studio, Mr. Egan?' Dan: 'Yeah. Yeah, you know, it's convenient.' 'Where do you live?' 'Nearby. Local... to here.'
jokes.setup: Dan has apparently joined Kent's Pilates studio to get access to him; Kent asks a simple question about where Dan lives.
jokes.punchline: Dan's deflecting non-answers ('Nearby. Local... to here.') reveal his cover story is terrible and he's visibly cornered.
jokes.explanation: Dan, the master manipulator, being cornered by Kent's simple logistics question is a great character reversal. 'Local... to here' is a lovely bit of faltering improv that shows Dan's game face slipping. The cringe is precise and well-earned.

jokes.text: Kent at Pilates: 'I hope you've done Pilates before. People think they can just get on one of these machines. No. Six months I did before I got on this.'
jokes.setup: Dan is trying to curry favor with Kent; Kent responds to the hostage situation comment by pivoting to Pilates safety protocols.
jokes.punchline: Kent treats Pilates preparation with the same obsessive precision he applies to national security, missing — or ignoring — the political conversation entirely.
jokes.explanation: Kent's complete deflection from national security to Pilates methodology is very funny character work. The specificity of 'six months' and the moral seriousness of machine preparation is perfect Kent Davison — his obsession with process applies to everything equally.

jokes.text: Jonah is ecstatic on Air Force Two, taking photos, asking Mike to take a picture of him looking out the window: 'Jonah. Jonah, calm down. It's a plane with a logo. It's not Space Mountain.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is having the time of his life aboard Air Force Two like a tourist.
jokes.punchline: Mike's Space Mountain comparison is perfectly deflating — reducing Air Force Two to an amusement park ride.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's childlike excitement about institutional symbols is a good character trait and Mike's 'Space Mountain' line is a clean deflation. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Jonah admits to Mike he has never been on Air Force One. Mike's exaggerated reaction: 'Oh!'
jokes.setup: Mike has just told Jonah not to get excited about Air Force Two — implying real power people have been on Air Force One.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's shamefaced confession that he's never been on AF1 deflates his entire performance of importance. Mike's 'Oh!' is an exaggerated stab of pity.
jokes.explanation: A decent callback beat. Jonah's status insecurity is a reliable comedy engine and this confirms he's lower on the hierarchy than he pretends. Mike's 'Oh!' lands as genuine mockery.

jokes.text: Mike's stoic philosophy on his financial disaster: 'I've changed the only thing I can... my reaction to things.' Selina: 'Oh. And your boxers occasionally, I hope.' Mike: 'I have a fresh pair every day now.' Selina: 'Oh, good for you, Mike.'
jokes.setup: Mike delivers a sincere Marcus Aurelius-style acceptance philosophy about his debts.
jokes.punchline: Selina's pivot to hygiene — implying Mike previously did not change his underwear daily — takes the zen wisdom and grounds it in filth.
jokes.explanation: A good Selina misdirect — she takes Mike's sincere philosophical statement and routes it through personal hygiene. 'I have a fresh pair every day now' confirms she was right to doubt, and her dry 'Oh, good for you, Mike' is perfectly patronizing. Solid character-to-character comedy.

jokes.text: Gary's melodrama setup: 'I know you think I'm melodramatic, ma'am.' Selina: 'I know you're melodramatic.' Gary: 'Uh-huh. Our world has just ended.'
jokes.setup: Gary acknowledges he tends to overreact.
jokes.punchline: He immediately proves the point by declaring 'Our world has just ended' — confirming his melodrama at the exact moment he claims self-awareness of it.
jokes.explanation: The structure here is very clean: acknowledgment → immediate self-betrayal → Selina's correction pre-empting his excuse. It's a three-beat joke that works because Gary's delivery almost certainly comes with total sincerity. The self-awareness-without-self-control is perfect characterization.

jokes.text: The blog post reveals Selina's signals gestictionary — Mike names it: 'I can't just tear up the gestictionary and come up with new codes like that.'
jokes.setup: The team needs to come up with new signals after their secret code was WikiLeaked.
jokes.punchline: Mike coins 'gestictionary' — a portmanteau of 'gesture' and 'dictionary' — treating their nonverbal code as a serious professional document.
jokes.explanation: 'Gestictionary' is a genuinely inventive portmanteau and it's even funnier for Mike saying it with complete seriousness — treating their absurd signals system as a sacred institutional document. Good wordplay with character weight.

jokes.text: Selina snaps her fingers: 'Okay, that could be a signal. Kind of a silent click.'
jokes.setup: The team is brainstorming new subtle exit signals.
jokes.punchline: Selina snaps her fingers — a completely obvious, unmissable gesture — and calls it 'a silent click,' completely missing that it's neither subtle nor clicking.
jokes.explanation: 'Silent click' for a finger snap is funny precisely because it's wrong — snaps are audible by definition. Selina's self-confident wrongness is good character work. Less memorable than the surrounding jokes.

jokes.text: Selina rejects a suggestion: 'No, that's like I squished a bug and then I said, Oh, it's so cool I squished a bug.'
jokes.setup: The team is proposing hand signals; Selina rejects one as too obvious.
jokes.punchline: Her example of what the gesture would look like is itself absurd — 'Oh, it's so cool I squished a bug' — which is such a specific and bizarre imaginary scenario that it's funnier than any criticism.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Selina's imaginary bug-squishing celebration is genuinely absurd and funny. It's the kind of ridiculous image that lands in the Veep register — odd but vivid.

jokes.text: Selina rejects the eyebrow rub: 'What if people think I have crabs in my eyebrows?' Mike: 'How can you have crabs in your eyebrow?'
jokes.setup: Mike suggests rubbing the eyebrow as a subtle signal.
jokes.punchline: Selina rejects it based on the fear of implying pubic lice in her eyebrows; Mike's literal biology question misses that 'eyebrow crabs' is a known phenomenon.
jokes.explanation: The crabs-in-eyebrows gag is a good absurdist escalation — the image is inherently funny and Selina's concern about it as a VP optics problem is a great character collision. Mike's deadpan biological question is the right coda.

jokes.text: Gary asks how crabs can be in an eyebrow; Selina explains: 'Okay, Gary, when a man and a woman love each other very much and it's the man's birthday...'
jokes.setup: Gary's innocent biology question opens the door to an explanation of how one acquires eyebrow crabs.
jokes.punchline: Selina begins the 'birds and bees' speech but immediately veers into the 'man's birthday' — implying the sex act that would cause this was a birthday favor — and stops there.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly constructed joke. 'When a man and a woman love each other very much' is the classic sex-ed preamble, but 'and it's the man's birthday' is the devastating, specific addition that implies exactly what birthday sex act produces eyebrow crabs without ever saying it. The interruption makes it even better — the audience fills in the rest. Economy is near-perfect: one clause tells you everything. This earns 8+ handily.

jokes.text: Mike: 'Crap in a hat.' Then: 'She's fine, but she's done something that'll make you want to kill her.'
jokes.setup: Mike is reacting to news about Catherine's essay.
jokes.punchline: 'Crap in a hat' as a substitute expletive is faintly absurd, and 'she's done something that'll make you want to kill her' is a brilliantly understated dark announcement.
jokes.explanation: Two quick beats — 'crap in a hat' is a slightly funny cleaned-up expletive, and the matter-of-fact announcement of Catherine's transgression is good deadpan understatement. Competent but not a standout.

jokes.text: Mike on Palestinian film: 'If Owen Wilson's not in it, I probably don't know it.' ... 'That doesn't sound like one of Owen's.'
jokes.setup: Amy explains the documentary is about nonviolent Palestinian resistance to Israeli settlement.
jokes.punchline: Mike evaluates the film purely on Owen Wilson's presence; after Amy describes its political content, he concludes it 'doesn't sound like one of Owen's' — as if Owen Wilson makes Palestinian resistance documentaries as a genre.
jokes.explanation: Mike's film taxonomy — does it or does it not contain Owen Wilson — is a brilliant character joke. The deadpan 'That doesn't sound like one of Owen's' implies Mike has catalogued Owen Wilson's filmography and cross-references it for documentary content. Perfect character-specific humor: Mike as a man whose pop cultural knowledge is both confident and deeply wrong.

jokes.text: Selina: 'All right, well, standard issue pro-Israeli, pro-Palestinian, but subtly more pro-Israeli statement. I mean, hit F9 and print that fucker out.'
jokes.setup: The team needs to respond to Catherine's pro-Palestinian essay.
jokes.punchline: Selina assumes there's a boilerplate statement — 'hit F9' — that is pre-loaded for Middle East crises, which is both a critique of political inauthenticity and a believable bureaucratic reality.
jokes.explanation: The F9 shortcut for a Middle East statement is a sharp satirical image — the notion that these delicate diplomatic positions are so formulaic they have a keyboard shortcut is a genuinely good satirical idea, delivered with the right contemptuous efficiency.

jokes.text: Amy: 'This needs to be the Gettysburg Address of tightrope-walking, say-nothing bullshit.' Gary: 'My time has come.'
jokes.setup: Amy articulates the impossible standard required for the statement.
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'My time has come' — delivered with apparent gravitas — implies he considers crafting vacuous political boilerplate to be his life's calling.
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'My time has come' is a perfect Gary moment. He hears 'Gettysburg Address of say-nothing bullshit' and responds as if he's been summoned by destiny — his pathetic eagerness to serve Selina has found its grandest expression in the task of crafting meaningless diplomatic boilerplate. The juxtaposition of Lincoln-level gravitas and total political emptiness is beautiful. Character-specific, economical, and quotable. Solid 8.

jokes.text: Mike: 'All right, next election I guess we lose Florida, then.'
jokes.setup: The team is dealing with Catherine's pro-Palestinian essay threatening the pro-Israel vote.
jokes.punchline: Mike's matter-of-fact projection — Florida's gone, moving on — is politically astute but delivered as casual resignation.
jokes.explanation: A competent political satire joke — Florida as the definitive Jewish-American swing vote is a real and recognizable observation. Mike's casual surrender is funny but not particularly original.

jokes.text: Selina at the pig roast: 'Walking the walk, talking the pork.' Then: 'That's a term I just coined today. Do you like it?' — referring to 'finger-licking fun.'
jokes.setup: Selina is working the crowd at the pig picking, performing rural warmth.
jokes.punchline: Her self-congratulation for coining 'finger-licking fun' — which is manifestly not a new phrase — is a wonderful bit of Selina obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: Selina attempting folksy warmth and proudly coining a phrase that has existed for decades (KFC literally owns it) is quintessential VP performance comedy. Her earnest self-congratulation makes it work.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Why am I the only one wearing a cowboy hat?' — visual gag, Selina standing alone in a cowboy hat looking ridiculous.
jokes.setup: Selina has been press-fleshing at the pig picking and wearing a cowboy hat.
jokes.punchline: She looks around and realizes she is the only one in a cowboy hat — which means someone gave her a prop hat to mark her as 'rural-friendly' and no actual rural person would wear one.
jokes.explanation: A good visual gag that punctures political costuming. The irony that real rural Americans don't actually wear cowboy hats at pig pickings while the VP has been dressed up like a Halloween costume is a pointed satire. Strong physical-comedy potential in the image.

jokes.text: Dan on the hog roast attendees: 'These people have a lot of flesh.'
jokes.setup: Someone (presumably Amy) is reporting to a superior that Selina is pressing the flesh with the crowd.
jokes.punchline: Dan's phone addition — 'these people have a lot of flesh' — is a casually brutal observation about the body types at a pork festival.
jokes.explanation: A quick, economical dark observation. 'Pressing the flesh' as a political phrase takes on new meaning at a pork festival among overweight attendees — the double meaning is not labored over, just noted and moved on. Clean.

jokes.text: Furlong: 'If this were the '60s, we could just have the National Guard shoot the daughter.'
jokes.setup: Furlong is discussing the Catherine essay problem and the difficulty of managing family-plus-Middle-East politics.
jokes.punchline: His solution — National Guard shooting — is historically specific (Kent State reference) and delivered as a genuine policy regret, which is terrifying and darkly funny.
jokes.explanation: Furlong's casual historical reference to Kent State as a viable political management tool is deeply dark and very in character — he genuinely misses this option. The historical specificity elevates it above a generic dark joke. Strong Furlong characterization.

jokes.text: Jonah parting the crowd with 'West Wing, West Wing. Pardon me. Coming through... Pardon me, West Wing. Pardon me. Excuse me.'
jokes.setup: Jonah needs to move through a crowd at the pig picking.
jokes.punchline: He uses 'West Wing' as if it's a VIP pass — announcing his institutional affiliation to random pig-picking attendees who have no idea what that means or why it should move them.
jokes.explanation: Jonah name-dropping 'West Wing' to clear a pig-roast crowd is a perfect distillation of his character — using institutional prestige as a social lubricant in contexts where it means nothing. His absolute faith in the brand is both pitiable and absurd.

jokes.text: Jonah tells Selina she needs to get Catherine to apologize 'unequivocally and immediately.' Selina: 'Yeah? Well, go shit yourself, Long Tall Sally...'
jokes.setup: Jonah relays POTUS's directive about Catherine with all the authority he can muster.
jokes.punchline: 'Long Tall Sally' is Selina's nickname for Jonah — a stinging reference to his height that she deploys immediately when challenged.
jokes.explanation: 'Long Tall Sally' is a great Jonah nickname — specific, demeaning, and musical-reference-inflected. Selina's pre-emptive 'go shit yourself' before the nickname is the right rhythm. Solid Veep insult comedy.

jokes.text: Selina to Catherine on phone: 'No, honey. Not you, Catherine. It was Mike. He had you on top of his body. No, that came out wrong.'
jokes.setup: Selina tries to explain to Catherine that she was watching Mike hold her phone while her image was displayed.
jokes.punchline: 'He had you on top of his body' sounds spectacularly wrong as a description of a phone screen, and Selina's immediate 'No, that came out wrong' acknowledges the accidental innuendo without fixing it.
jokes.explanation: The accidental-innuendo-with-immediate-self-correction is a reliable comedy structure, and 'he had you on top of his body' is genuinely unfortunate enough to land. Selina's horrified self-correction doesn't help and that's the right move.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'His name is Rahim. He's Dilka's brother.' Selina: 'Dilka's brother?' Catherine: 'What? Do you have a problem with him being Iranian?' Selina: 'Okay, Catherine, you know that I am not a racist, okay? My boyfriend in college was a quarter Cherokee.'
jokes.setup: Catherine reveals her new boyfriend is Iranian.
jokes.punchline: Selina's declaration that she's not racist is immediately undercut by 'My boyfriend in college was a quarter Cherokee' — the qualifier 'a quarter' doing enormous work to reveal the limits of her tolerance.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly constructed character joke. 'A quarter Cherokee' is the key — Selina's tolerance extends to someone who was 75% white, and she deploys this as proof of open-mindedness. The fraction is doing all the work: it simultaneously shows she's keeping score and that her score is pitifully low. Character-specific (only Selina would cite this arithmetic), earned, and quotable. Strong 8.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I'll bring you back a sandwich and a six-toed husband.' (to Gary's aide about the pig picking)
jokes.setup: An aide's father is ill so she's skipping the pig picking.
jokes.punchline: Selina's throwaway 'six-toed husband' comment equates rural North Carolina with inbreeding, tossed off with genuine cheerfulness.
jokes.explanation: A quick, casually offensive joke that works because Selina says it while performing concern for the aide's family — the tonal whiplash is very sharp. 'Six-toed husband' is a memorable specific detail for the joke.

jokes.text: Furlong: 'I'm running out of nice juice to spray at these shit munchers.'
jokes.setup: Furlong is trying to be pleasant at the pig picking for political purposes.
jokes.punchline: 'Nice juice' as a depleting resource, deployed on 'shit munchers,' is a perfect Furlong construction — the effort of niceness rendered as a finite industrial spray.
jokes.explanation: One of the great Furlong lines. 'Nice juice' is an inventive coinage for social politeness — something you have a tank of and can run dry. 'Shit munchers' for the voters he's supposed to court is brutally perfect. The combination of the industrial metaphor with the vulgar noun is a genuinely original compound insult. Character-specific: only Furlong would articulate his social capacity this way. Earns 8.

jokes.text: Dan and Amy's 'dying dad' scam — Dan reveals: 'That's my dad. Jesus, he doesn't look like he's dying.' Amy: 'My family was guilt-tripping the shit out of me and so I exaggerated to come visit. Besides, a dying dad can be a get-out-of-jail-free card. Like with Furlong.'
jokes.setup: Amy told people her father was dying to escape Furlong and bring Dan to help with the statement.
jokes.punchline: Her father is perfectly fine; she fabricated his dying to extract herself from a political situation and Dan from Furlong.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Amy faked her dad's dying is a great character moment — it shows her calculating ruthlessness while also revealing her family dynamics in a single beat. 'A dying dad can be a get-out-of-jail-free card' is a genuinely sharp piece of political insider cynicism.

jokes.text: Dan, genuinely moved: 'That is cold. Also kind of hot.'
jokes.setup: Amy reveals her dying-dad deception.
jokes.punchline: Dan's immediate pivot from moral judgment to sexual attraction — 'Also kind of hot' — is a perfect Dan Egan reaction to Amy's Machiavellian behavior.
jokes.explanation: Economy is near-perfect: four words do two things — acknowledge the moral dimension and immediately abandon it for attraction. 'Also kind of hot' is exactly what Dan would think, said aloud with zero filter. The Dan-Amy relationship history makes this land harder.

jokes.text: Furlong to his aide, about Dan and Amy leaving: 'It's a metaphor.' Aide: 'I don't think it strictly is, sir.' Furlong: 'As a figure of speech ...' Aide: 'You know, you're about as annoying as a condom filled with fire ants. How's that for a fucking metaphor?' Aide: 'That's a simile, sir.' Furlong: 'Shut your mouth, you fat girl.'
jokes.setup: Furlong uses 'I've got all the time in the world' and insists it's a metaphor; his aide gently corrects him.
jokes.punchline: The sequence escalates: Furlong attempts to demonstrate his mastery of metaphor with the 'condom filled with fire ants' image; the aide correctly identifies it as a simile; Furlong responds with a fat insult.
jokes.explanation: This is the strongest joke sequence in the episode and competes for best in the series. The architecture is perfect: (1) Furlong wrongly calls his phrase a metaphor, (2) aide correctly corrects, (3) Furlong's 'demonstration' of metaphor is a surreal, specific, and disgusting image ('condom filled with fire ants') that is objectively a simile, (4) aide correctly corrects AGAIN, (5) Furlong descends into a random fat insult. The 'condom filled with fire ants' is a genuinely original piece of invective — specific enough to visualize and revolting enough to register. The grammar instruction embedded in a power dynamic is Veep at its finest. Every beat earns its laugh. Near the 9+ anchor territory.

jokes.text: Dan to Amy after she says her dad is at George Washington hospital: 'Oh, that's a good one. They got a big Starbucks there. It's nice. You know, people go in there that aren't...' Amy: 'You don't have to speak.' Dan: 'Thank you.'
jokes.setup: Amy names the hospital where her 'dying' dad supposedly is.
jokes.punchline: Dan tries to say something supportive about the hospital but gets lost trying to complete 'people go in there that aren't...' — implying 'dying' — and Amy mercifully cuts him off. His 'Thank you' accepts the rescue with relief.
jokes.explanation: Dan's earlier line about not being great with empathy ('I'm not really great with illness or death or basic empathy') pays off perfectly here as he flounders trying to say something kind. The truncated sentence and Amy's merciful interruption followed by Dan's grateful 'Thank you' is very economically structured cringe.

jokes.text: Dan: 'George Washington. Oh, that's a good one. They got a big Starbucks there. It's nice. You know, people go in there that aren't... You don't have to speak.'
jokes.setup: Amy's 'dying' father is at George Washington hospital
jokes.punchline: Dan tries to find something comforting to say about a hospital and lands on the Starbucks — then attempts to recover by noting that the Starbucks serves people who 'aren't...' (dying) before abandoning the sentence entirely
jokes.explanation: Dan's emotional incompetence is perfectly rendered here. He genuinely tries — the Starbucks recommendation is a real attempt at comfort — and then the unfinished sentence ('people go in there that aren't...') reveals him trying to say 'dying' and bottling it. 'You don't have to speak' as self-advice is a perfect button. This is Dan in miniature: amoral, self-aware enough to know he's failing, unable to do better. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Mike's helicopter decapitation fear: 'It's just getting in. I'm afraid it's gonna wobble. Like, you know, it'd chop the top of my head off like a soft-boiled egg and then I'm, like, running around trying to put my head back on.'
jokes.setup: Mike has to take a helicopter to get Catherine.
jokes.punchline: Mike's phobia is described in loving, absurd detail — the soft-boiled egg metaphor and the image of headless Mike 'trying to put his head back on' is a vivid nightmare scenario.
jokes.explanation: The soft-boiled egg decapitation metaphor is genuinely inventive and the image of headless Mike running around is good absurdist comedy. Slightly overlong but the specific image is memorable.

jokes.text: Mike, trying to make conversation with Rahim: 'Right, Rahim?' about the Middle East being 'very confusing.' Rahim: 'No, sometimes it can seem pretty clear-cut to me.'
jokes.setup: Mike attempts to defuse the Palestinian film controversy by calling the Middle East 'very confusing.'
jokes.punchline: Rahim — an Iranian-American — calmly states it can seem clear-cut to him, which makes Mike's sweeping 'confusion' defense obviously self-serving.
jokes.explanation: Rahim's quiet but pointed response exposes Mike's diplomatic hand-waving for what it is. Good cringe beat — Mike is undone by the person he's trying to neutralize.

jokes.text: Jonah on the phone to Mike about pork and Jewish sensitivity: 'Most Jews in this country are fine with pork. Pork schmork, they often say.' — then repeats it to Kent.
jokes.setup: Mike is alarmed at the visual of Selina endorsing the Jewish homeland next to a pig roast.
jokes.punchline: Jonah dismisses the sensitivity with 'Pork schmork' — a Yiddish-ish dismissal of Yiddish dietary laws, which he then repeats directly to Kent Davison.
jokes.explanation: 'Pork schmork' is a genuinely brilliant construction — using Yiddish's own phonological pattern to dismiss a Jewish dietary concern is linguistically self-aware and deeply oblivious simultaneously. Jonah repeating it to a presumably Jewish political operative is the escalation that makes it exceptional. 'Pork schmork, they often say' implies this is a widespread Jewish saying, which it is absolutely not.

jokes.text: Jonah to Mike: 'Most Jews in this country are fine with pork. Pork schmork, they often say.'
jokes.setup: Mike tells Jonah to move Selina away from the roasting pig due to Israeli optics
jokes.punchline: Jonah's dismissal invents the phrase 'pork schmork' — a Yiddish-inflected diminutive — and attributes it to 'most Jews in this country' with complete confidence and zero basis
jokes.explanation: 'Pork schmork, they often say' is an invented folk wisdom attributed to an entire religious community with zero evidence. The Yiddish construction ('schmork') is perfectly inappropriate — using Jewish linguistic heritage to dismiss Jewish dietary laws. The confidence of the attribution ('they often say') is the signature Jonah move. Justifies 8.3.

jokes.text: Kent to Jonah: 'You ever hear of the Old Testament, Mike?' — calling Jonah 'Mike.'
jokes.setup: Jonah has just told Kent that most Jews are fine with pork.
jokes.punchline: Kent's Bible citation is delivered to 'Mike' — he's misidentified Jonah as Mike, which is both a cut (you're not even worth knowing) and a demonstration of his detached indifference.
jokes.explanation: The misidentification is a small but characteristically devastating Kent detail — Jonah is so unimportant to Kent that he doesn't bother learning his name. The two-layer joke (Old Testament correction + wrong name) is efficient.

jokes.text: Amy to Dan: 'I'm not really great with illness or death or basic empathy, but I just want to say I'm sorry about your dad and I think you're being really brave.'
jokes.setup: Amy tries to offer Dan sympathy about his supposedly dying father.
jokes.punchline: Her disclaimer 'I'm not really great with illness or death or basic empathy' is so honest and self-aware that it undermines the comfort it's meant to provide — she's essentially confessing her empathy is performative.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully written character joke. The self-sabotaging caveat — listing the exact things you need for comforting someone and admitting you're bad at all of them — is both recognizable (we've all done a version of this) and a specific Amy trait. 'Basic empathy' is the key addition that makes it exceptional. Then the reveal that Dan's dad is fine turns this into retroactive dramatic irony.

jokes.text: The hog-roast pig-blocking logistics: Jonah being asked to physically block the pig from view during Selina's pro-Israel statement; the hot coals problem; 'quit touching the pig, it's not a fucking petting zoo'; 'that fucker is skinned and roasted. He's at a huge disadvantage.'
jokes.setup: Selina is giving a pro-Israel statement while standing next to a roasted pig — the visual is politically devastating.
jokes.punchline: The escalating attempts to block the pig culminate in a soldier saying 'He's at a huge disadvantage' about the roasted pig — which is both objectively true and absurdly literal.
jokes.explanation: The extended pig-blocking sequence has escalating absurdity — the bureaucratic seriousness applied to visually hiding a dead pig is good Veep cringe. 'He's at a huge disadvantage' is the best line, a perfect deadpan literal reading of a roasted pig's threat level.

jokes.text: Furlong's motorcade line: 'I don't care if there is a funeral procession due to use that street. It is to be closed by 4:00 today.' Aide: 'It'll cheer the family up, sir. Everyone loves a motorcade.'
jokes.setup: Furlong is ordering a street closure regardless of other events.
jokes.punchline: Will's suggestion that a motorcade would cheer a grieving funeral procession is delivered with such bureaucratic sincerity that it's both absurd and somehow plausible in this world.
jokes.explanation: Will's line is delivered with pure sincerity and it's funnier for it — the idea that political pomp would console the bereaved is absurdly insulting and Will means it well. Good economy and the dark subversion is earned.

jokes.text: Sue's confrontation with Furlong: Furlong storms in demanding a meeting, is rebuffed. 'My wife ... I'm not looking for sympathy for this ... but she was very ill with kidney disease.' Sue: 'Your wife is perfectly fine. She ran the Seattle Marathon.'
jokes.setup: Furlong tries to use his wife's illness as a sympathy play to get access to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Sue has researched this and shuts it down instantly with specific evidence — the Seattle Marathon.
jokes.explanation: Sue is at her most formidable here. The specificity of 'She ran the Seattle Marathon' is the key — Sue has Furlong's personal life on file and can deploy it as a weapon. It's a perfect Sue moment: impassive, devastating, and instantly deflating. Furlong's sympathy play is swatted away with one sentence. The Seattle Marathon detail is the right level of specificity — real enough to be verifiable, obscure enough to be humiliating. Strong 8.

jokes.text: Furlong's meltdown at Sue: 'All right, don't fuck with me, honey. Just put my fucking name in your stupid fucking schedule and get me a face-to-face with the veep now!' followed by 'Okay, I've got boxes and boxes on Selina Meyer. You understand me? I've got Pandora's fucking boxes on her.'
jokes.setup: Furlong's polite-politician facade has completely collapsed after Sue's Seattle Marathon deflation.
jokes.punchline: 'Pandora's fucking boxes' — plural — is a wonderful malapropism/escalation. Pandora's box is singular; the plural with 'fucking' inserted reveals Furlong trying to sound menacing and educated simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Furlong's facade crumbling is good character comedy, and 'Pandora's fucking boxes' is a great piece of inadvertent comedy — he's trying to sound threatening and erudite but pluralizing a singular myth and inserting profanity undermines both aims.

jokes.text: Farmer on pig excrement: 'You can't raise pigs without excrement, ma'am. It just can't be done. They defecate. And they defecate with speed. And volume.'
jokes.setup: Selina is listening politely to a pork farmer describe the challenges of their industry.
jokes.punchline: The farmer's advocacy — delivered with the gravity of a Senate testimony — culminates in 'And volume.' The word 'volume' used for pig shit is a perfect bureaucratic understatement.
jokes.explanation: The three-beat escalation — 'They defecate. And they defecate with speed. And volume.' — is well-constructed comedy through rhythm. 'Volume' as the final word is the right choice: it's the most mundanely technical word for maximum quantity of feces. Selina's 'Oh, Lord. Oh, goodness' and 'Why wouldn't they?' are good reaction beats.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I've been scratching my eyebrow for three and a half fucking hours. Where have you been?' / Gary: 'I thought we weren't doing the eyebrow thing. You said about the crabs.'
jokes.setup: Selina has been using the eyebrow signal to no avail.
jokes.punchline: Gary's defense — he thought the crabs concern vetoed the eyebrow signal — shows he took her absurd objection literally and acted on it for hours.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the crabs objection — which the audience has since forgotten — lands with force because it reveals Gary genuinely overrode the signal based on Selina's hypothetical eyebrow crabs concern. 'Three and a half fucking hours' is the precise misery quantity, and Gary's reasoned explanation for his failure ('you said about the crabs') is perfectly calibrated idiocy. Strong structural callback.

jokes.text: Selina has been scratching her eyebrow for three and a half hours: 'I've been scratching my eyebrow for three and a half fucking hours. Where have you been?' Mike: 'I thought we weren't doing the eyebrow thing.'
jokes.setup: Selina established the eyebrow signal and has been using it to signal Mike for an exit; Mike never came.
jokes.punchline: Mike thought they'd decided against the eyebrow signal (because of the crabs conversation), so Selina has been frantically scratching for hours with no rescue.
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback payoff. The crabs/eyebrow signal debate from the plane is paid off with Selina having scratched her eyebrow for three and a half hours while Mike, reasonably, thought they weren't using it. The specificity of 'three and a half fucking hours' is the right level of hyperbolic complaint. Mike's confusion is earned by the earlier scene. Good structural work across the episode.

jokes.text: Selina: 'She's getting her leak on.'
jokes.setup: The team has realized Dana (Mike's girlfriend) leaked the signals.
jokes.punchline: 'Getting her leak on' is a rap/R&B phrase construction ('getting her freak on') applied to political leaking — a jarring register clash from the VP.
jokes.explanation: A quick wordplay moment — the slang construction from a VP is a good register joke. Brief and efficient.

jokes.text: The pig fat jacket: 'What in the living fuck is that god-awful smell, Jonah?' Jonah: 'Oh, my jacket is partially soaked in pork fat, ma'am.'
jokes.setup: Jonah was ordered to block the pig with his body, which was near hot coals.
jokes.punchline: The consequence of this absurd task is Jonah now smells catastrophically of pork fat — which he explains with complete matter-of-fact acceptance.
jokes.explanation: A good payoff to the pig-blocking subplot. 'Partially soaked in pork fat' is a funny and specific description of Jonah's state. His casual acceptance of the situation is good character work.

jokes.text: Dan on Amy: 'Amy and I just weren't compatible.' Amy: 'Right. It turns out I can't mate with anyone outside of my species.'
jokes.setup: Dan diplomatically says they weren't compatible.
jokes.punchline: Amy's retort — 'I can't mate with anyone outside of my species' — implies Dan is a different (lesser) species. The use of 'mate' rather than 'date' is the specific word choice that elevates the insult.
jokes.explanation: Amy's line is sharp because 'mate' is doing double work — it's the biological term that implies Dan is a different species AND has the sexual connotation of the context. The insult is both an elevation of herself and a dehumanization of Dan. Very economical. Character-specific for Amy's prickly precision.

jokes.text: Selina: 'We all know what you need, Amy. I mean besides a big, fat slap.'
jokes.setup: The group is debating Amy's stated preference not to have children.
jokes.punchline: Selina's suggestion of what Amy 'needs' pivots to 'a big, fat slap' — an aggressive dismissal of Amy's stated preferences dressed as teasing.
jokes.explanation: A casually dark Selina line. 'Besides a big, fat slap' as a secondary need is abrupt and funny precisely because it undermines the affectionate framing. Effective but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Amy: 'I do not want children, okay? Why can nobody accept this?' Multiple people: 'Sweetie, you do not have to decide right now.' Then Furlong's aide: 'I remained fertile till I was 56.'
jokes.setup: Amy asserts she doesn't want children and asks why no one accepts it.
jokes.punchline: The response ignores her stated position entirely — treating it as indecision rather than a decision — then escalates with the completely unsolicited fertility disclosure 'I remained fertile till I was 56.'
jokes.explanation: The 'I remained fertile till I was 56' is an overshare that arrives at exactly the wrong moment — the speaker hears the anxiety about time and offers their own fertility timeline as comfort. The absurdity is in its total irrelevance to what Amy needs. Good cringe escalation.

jokes.text: Selina: 'At work we actually call her Amiable Amy.'
jokes.setup: People are telling Amy they just want her to be happy, implying she isn't.
jokes.punchline: Selina claims they call her 'Amiable Amy' — which is so clearly false given what we've seen that it lands as deadpan cruelty.
jokes.explanation: A nicely delivered ironic nickname. 'Amiable Amy' is such an obviously wrong description that the false-affirmation is a backhanded slap. Dan's later improvement ('Amy Whitehouse') is the better version of this joke (id: 79).

jokes.text: Dan: 'I think I'm gonna go get a coffee. Anyone else?' Father: 'No, thank you.' Selina: 'I will help you carry that second coffee.'
jokes.setup: Dan needs to extract himself from the family situation.
jokes.punchline: Selina's offer to 'help carry the second coffee' is a transparent excuse to leave — neither of them drinks two coffees; the pretense is hilariously thin and everyone in the room knows it.
jokes.explanation: A quick exit-pretense joke. 'Help carry that second coffee' is the right level of transparent excuse — it's technically possible but implausible. The social awkwardness is efficiently captured.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'This is gonna be like Super Size Me all over again.' about Selina planning to watch '5 Broken Cameras' — followed by 'Why don't you guys just go see Mamma Mia! again for the 10th fucking time?'
jokes.setup: Selina says she'll watch Catherine's Palestinian documentary.
jokes.punchline: Catherine predicts her mother will watch it with the shallow engagement she brings to all culture — 'Super Size Me' level engagement — then reveals the devastating detail that her mother has seen Mamma Mia! ten times.
jokes.explanation: The 'Mamma Mia! 10 times' detail is the right specific number and film. Selina's cultural taste being ABBA musicals at the expense of Palestinian documentaries is a perfect character portrait. Catherine's contempt is earned and the joke is specific to this relationship.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I want to see the film and I will see the film. Gary, we need to get it. It's Five Easy Pieces.' Gary: 'It's 5 Broken Cameras.' Selina: 'Okay, it's Broken Cameras.'
jokes.setup: Selina commits to watching Catherine's film.
jokes.punchline: She immediately gets the title wrong (confusing it with Five Easy Pieces), then when corrected gets it wrong again ('Broken Cameras'), suggesting she will not watch this film.
jokes.explanation: 'Five Easy Pieces' is a great wrong title — it's a real classic film, a reasonable mistake, but one that reveals Selina's cultural frame. Her second attempt dropping to just 'Broken Cameras' suggests the title information is already fading. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Selina negotiating: 'Thursday you're with me and Grandma. Friday you can be with the new boyfriend, okay? And Saturday. If you come home on Saturday night.' Catherine: 'Yeah, I'll check that over with Rahim.'
jokes.setup: Selina is negotiating holiday time with her daughter
jokes.punchline: Catherine deferring to her boyfriend on the proposed schedule is Selina's nightmare — her daughter now treats the new Iranian boyfriend as an equal co-negotiator
jokes.explanation: A functional beat that sets up the Rahim subplot's next phase. Catherine's 'I'll check with Rahim' lands as a parental horror moment for Selina.

jokes.text: Amy reports: 'Sue apparently bitch-slapped Furlong. He usually has to pay for that.'
jokes.setup: Amy delivers the news that Sue dealt with Furlong
jokes.punchline: 'He usually has to pay for that' — implying Furlong is a client of S&M services and considers being hit by women a luxury he normally pays for
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the Furlong/Sue confrontation. The joke implies a rich, specific, and disturbing personal life for Furlong. 'He usually has to pay for that' has a wonderful rhythm. Quotable.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Amiable Amy? That is the best you could come up with?' Amy: 'Yeah, you know, I knew I should have gone with Amy Whitehouse.'
jokes.setup: Dan privately critiques Selina's nickname choice for Amy.
jokes.punchline: Selina's self-critique — she should have used 'Amy Winehouse' — invokes the late singer, whose death-adjacent name tag is a far crueler nickname for Amy than 'Amiable.'
jokes.explanation: The callback to the 'Amiable Amy' nickname with Selina's self-improvement — 'Amy Whitehouse' — is excellent. The dark implication (Amy Winehouse died young of addiction) makes it a devastating upgrade. Selina's regret that she didn't use it is funnier than if she'd said it directly. Good callback structure.

tmdb_overview: Selina attends a pig roast in North Carolina as part of the president's "Listen to Rural America" initiative. Dan takes up Pilates to get face time.
insight: Sixty-one jokes in 38 minutes: character chaos sustains momentum despite uneven punchlines.
